name: Jean Noblet Tarot — The Oldest Surviving (Near-Complete) Tarot de Marseille Deck (Paris, c. 1650)
description:
The **oldest surviving complete Tarot de Marseille deck** (the uncut Cary Sheet, c. 1500, is earlier evidence of the *pattern* itself), printed in Paris c. 1650 by Jean Noblet — **110 years before Conver**. The single known copy lives at the **Bibliothèque nationale de France** and is on **Gallica** (ark:/12148/btv1b105109641), freely usable. Its siblings — the Jacques Viéville deck (ark:/12148/btv1b10510963k) and an anonymous Parisian tarot (ark:/12148/btv1b105109624) — sit right alongside it: three mid-17th-century Parisian decks just upstream of the Marseille standard.

ANCESTRAL STATUS: **ancestral** to the Marseille standard (the oldest extant TdM of the C-order line).

SOURCES: BnF / Gallica; Thierry Depaulis.

---
*AI-assisted first-pass draft, pending review by the maintainer and the Tarot History Forum. Corrections welcome via pending edit.*

**RESEARCH NOTE (2026-06-13).** Strictly **near-complete** (the Six–Ten of Swords are missing); 'complete' here distinguishes it from the Cary *sheet* (an uncut partial sheet), but the precise wording is a maintainer call. BnF dates the surviving object **1659** (Noblet active c.1659–1681), so 'c. 1650' is soft — read as c.1650/1659. It is the Type I Marseille (vs Conver's Type II). See research/decks/noblet-tarot.md.

**EDITORIAL CALL (2026-06-13, maintainer-delegated):** title changed 'Complete' → 'Near-Complete' — the pack is missing the Six–Ten of Swords (BnF/WOPC). Still the oldest surviving TdM deck and the Type I reference. [@web_wopc_noblet; @web_bnf_noblet]

sections.What it is: Jean Noblet's Paris deck, c. 1650 — the earliest surviving Tarot de Marseille, one known copy at the BnF (Gallica).

image_credit: Bibliothèque nationale de France, Paris (Jean Noblet, c. 1650)
